scale.note: descriptors[] ordered Level 1 (least mature) -> Level 5 (most mature); index+1 = points. reverse:true flips to 5..1 for negatively-keyed items. Each assessment draws askCount distinct factors per dimension and asks one random question from each.

questions.text: Which statement best captures how confident your employees are applying everyday security practices?
questions.guidance: Measures everyday self-efficacy: employees' confidence in carrying out routine secure behaviours.
descriptors: They feel out of their depth and avoid security tasks where they can.
They're confident only when there's a step-by-step instruction to follow.
They're confident with the tasks we've trained them on, like reporting phishing.
They handle most day-to-day security decisions on their own without checking.
Their confidence stays current as tools change, kept fresh by practice and feedback.

questions.text: An employee hits an unfamiliar, high-pressure situation the training never covered. What's their likely response?
questions.guidance: Measures resilience-related self-efficacy: confidence when conditions fall outside routine.
descriptors: They freeze, guess, or disengage entirely.
They cope only if a rule happens to fit; otherwise they take a risky shortcut.
They manage a pressured version of a familiar threat, but a truly novel one throws them.
They stay composed and apply sound judgement, drawing on experience rather than a rule.
They respond confidently to the unexpected, expecting to handle new threats, not just familiar ones.

questions.text: Asked whether tools like MFA and phishing reporting really make a difference, most employees would say:
questions.guidance: Measures response efficacy: belief that recommended security measures actually reduce risk.
descriptors: “Not really, breaches happen anyway, and that's IT's problem.”
“We use them because we have to, not because they help.”
“Yes, they reduce specific risks in my work, and I understand why.”
“Definitely, I've seen them catch real threats.”
“Absolutely, we're regularly shown the measurable difference they make.”

questions.text: How do employees relate to the security decisions in their role?
questions.guidance: Measures the employee's felt sense of agency over security decisions.
descriptors: As someone else's job, with no real control or responsibility.
As rules to follow because they're required, with no sense of ownership.
As choices they make through reflection once they understand the reasoning.
As their own responsibility: they believe their choices affect outcomes.
As proactive risk decisions they own and act on independently.

questions.text: When an employee wants to do the secure thing, how easy does your environment make it?
questions.guidance: Measures the organisational side of agency: whether the environment removes friction.
descriptors: Hard: friction and obstacles push them toward workarounds.
We add controls to meet requirements without caring how hard they are to follow.
We've removed the most obvious obstacles in common situations.
The secure choice is usually the easy choice, and we simplify based on feedback.
Secure-by-default pathways let people decide confidently with minimal effort.

questions.text: What mainly drives your employees to follow security policies?
questions.guidance: Measures whether secure behaviour is intrinsically or extrinsically motivated.
descriptors: Nothing much; they feel no connection to it at all.
Avoiding punishment or ticking the mandatory box.
A growing sense that secure behaviour is worthwhile and responsible.
Seeing it as part of their professional identity, reinforced by peers.
Genuine belief that protecting the organisation matters, whether or not anyone's watching.

questions.text: Asked whether breaking the policy is “wrong” even if no one finds out, most employees would feel:
questions.guidance: Measures whether compliance is driven by an internalised ethical sense of right and wrong.
descriptors: It's not wrong at all; breaches are normal and harmless.
It's risky or punishable, but not really wrong.
It's probably the irresponsible thing to do, though convenience can win.
It's wrong and against their integrity; they'd feel uncomfortable doing it.
It's a clear breach of duty; they uphold and advocate for doing the right thing.

questions.text: When an employee justifies skipping a step with excuses such as “I was busy” or “it was harmless”, what does your organisation do?
questions.guidance: Measures how well the organisation surfaces and counters neutralisation rationalisations.
descriptors: Nothing. These excuses go unchallenged.
We rely on rules and penalties, which the excuses easily get around.
We explain the reasons behind the rules, which takes the wind out of some excuses.
We name these exact excuses in training and messages, and challenge them head on.
We plan for these excuses and counter them with well-timed prompts and realistic scenarios, so they no longer hold up.

questions.text: How much of a mental burden do your security rules put on employees?
questions.guidance: Measures the cognitive dimension of security fatigue.
descriptors: Too much. People are swamped and routinely ignore what's asked of them.
Heavy. They cope by cutting corners, like reusing passwords.
Manageable, but the sheer number of alerts and training sessions wears people down.
Light. We've simplified our processes and cut out unnecessary alerts.
Very low. Automation and sensible defaults keep the effort small and attention high.

questions.text: How do employees feel about the constant stream of security demands?
questions.guidance: Measures the emotional dimension of security fatigue.
descriptors: Fed up or resentful. They've switched off entirely.
They go along with it grudgingly. Goodwill is low and frustration is building.
Generally willing, but weary when demands feel repetitive or unexplained.
Mostly fine. We space demands out and explain the reasons behind them.
Comfortable. The demands feel fair, so people stay willing to engage over the long run.

questions.text: Asked about their own odds of being hit by a cyberattack, most employees would say:
questions.guidance: Measures optimism bias about personal risk.
descriptors: “That happens to other people, not me.”
“I'm experienced, so I'm less likely than my colleagues to fall for it.”
“Attacks happen here, but I'm probably not a target.”
“Anyone can be targeted, including me.”
“I'm as much a target as anyone, and I stay alert without being paranoid.”

questions.text: A warning or MFA prompt appears mid-task. What does the typical employee do?
questions.guidance: Measures habituation to in-the-moment operational security cues.
descriptors: Dismisses it automatically; it's just background noise now.
Clicks through as fast as possible to get on with work.
Usually notices its importance, though attention slips with frequency.
Pauses to assess it before acting, prompted by well-designed cues.
Reliably attends to genuine warnings without being numbed by routine ones.

questions.text: How do employees engage with your ongoing security communications?
questions.guidance: Measures habituation to awareness communications over time.
descriptors: They ignore them entirely.
They skim or dismiss them as routine corporate noise.
They value them, but attention fades as messages repeat.
They engage because content is varied, relevant, and well-timed.
They stay attentive long-term as we continuously refresh the messaging.

questions.text: When it comes to mental shortcuts shaping security decisions, your employees are:
questions.guidance: Measures individual awareness of cognitive bias in security decisions.
descriptors: Unaware: judgements are automatic and bias drives risky choices.
Vaguely aware, but it changes nothing in practice.
Learning to spot common decision traps as we make risks more concrete.
Actively questioning their assumptions in risky moments.
Consistently catching themselves and pausing for deliberate evaluation.

questions.text: How well does your organisation set things up so people make safe choices without having to think hard?
questions.guidance: Measures whether the organisation designs its processes and messages to steer people toward safe choices, rather than leaving it to willpower.
descriptors: It doesn't. Processes ignore how people actually make decisions.
It mentions these mental shortcuts in training but changes nothing about how things are set up.
It's starting to make risks clearer and remove the obvious traps.
It uses prompts, realistic scenarios, and small design changes to steer better decisions.
It builds the safe choice into the way things work by default, so people don't have to rely on willpower.

questions.text: An employee clicks a phishing link. When does any learning reach them?
questions.guidance: Measures immediacy: whether correction lands at the teachable moment.
descriptors: Never: it triggers incident response but no learning for them.
Days later, as a reminder to finish the annual module; the moment's gone.
Right away, through a simulation landing page, at least for phishing.
Immediately, across many risk scenarios, not just phishing.
Before they even click: early signals trigger a micro-intervention.

questions.text: When an employee makes a security mistake, is the corrective training a one-off, or is it repeated and varied over time?
questions.guidance: Measures whether correction is repeated and varied to build habit.
descriptors: It's a one-off, with no follow-up or repetition.
At most a single retrospective reminder, no cycle.
It's timely but not yet repeated or varied over time.
Cycles are spaced and varied to build habit, with repeats tracked.
Frequency, format, and content adapt to individual and group data.

questions.text: Our security learning is delivered:
questions.guidance: Measures cadence: whether learning is distributed across the year.
descriptors: Only after incidents, with no cadence.
In one or two big blocks a year, nothing in between.
As primary training plus periodic micro-content across the year.
Continuously, varied and spaced, so it's present in daily work.
With frequency and timing adapting in real time to data and threats.

questions.text: How do you decide how much reinforcement is enough?
questions.guidance: Measures calibration to engagement and fatigue signals.
descriptors: We don't; there's no sense that knowledge decays or that we'd monitor it.
Delivery is fixed and uniform regardless of exposure.
A cadence exists but isn't calibrated to engagement or fatigue.
We track engagement and disengagement and adjust to avoid decay and fatigue.
Intensity is managed per individual and group using fatigue and engagement data.

questions.text: How is content broken up and delivered?
questions.guidance: Measures chunking and distribution of content.
descriptors: Only when a problem forces it, with no structure.
In large annual or semi-annual blocks.
Beginning to be split into shorter units at regular intervals.
Consistently in brief, varied units across the year.
With unit frequency and sequencing personalised to individual data.

questions.text: Our approach of delivering security content in small, regular pieces over time is designed to:
questions.guidance: Measures whether delivery targets spaced repetition and habit formation.
descriptors: Nothing deliberate; we don't think in terms of repetition over time.
Deliver information once and assume it sticks.
Space content out, though not yet around habit formation.
Reinforce the same core behaviours until responses become instinctive.
Produce measurable habit formation, not just sustained awareness.

questions.text: Which best describes your security communications as a whole?
questions.guidance: Measures campaign mechanics: cadence, format variety, identity.
descriptors: Ad hoc, only after incidents or deadlines.
Uniform policy reminders people filter out.
Multi-channel with a recognisable identity and a monthly cadence.
Run like an internal brand campaign, varied and sustained without becoming noise.
With channel, format, and cadence adapting in real time to data.

questions.text: How is your security messaging framed?
questions.guidance: Measures audience-relevant framing versus uniform obligation.
descriptors: Without thought for how it lands on different people.
As uniform compliance notices, not designed to earn attention.
Beginning to be framed around relevance, though tailoring is limited.
Calibrated to different segments and cultures, refined by engagement data.
With a demonstrable link between message quality and behaviour across segments.

questions.text: If you use gamification, what does it actually do?
questions.guidance: Measures whether gamification builds real skill versus cosmetic mechanics.
descriptors: There's none; training is passive or lecture-style.
Cosmetic badges or progress bars on otherwise passive content.
Scenario-based content with immediate feedback that requires decisions.
Realistic, varied scenarios that build genuine decision-making skill.
Adaptive scenarios with demonstrable gains in threat recognition.

questions.text: How does gamification fit into the wider program and different audiences?
questions.guidance: Measures integration into reinforcement and audience fit.
descriptors: There's nothing to integrate.
Any mechanics stand alone and are uniform across audiences.
It's an engagement tool, but not yet embedded or tailored.
It's part of a continuous program, adjusted where it underperforms by segment.
Fully integrated into reinforcement and tuned to audience response.

questions.text: When employees participate in simulation exercises (like fake phishing emails), how realistic are they?
questions.guidance: Measures realism and immediate corrective feedback.
descriptors: We don't run any, so people meet real threats with no practice first.
We run the odd basic test for the numbers, but people get little or no feedback.
We run them regularly, and people find out straight away when they get something wrong.
They feel realistic, come in different forms, and always give quick feedback.
We keep improving them, and we can show people are getting better at spotting threats.

questions.text: How much do your simulations vary over time?
questions.guidance: Measures variation and validity to prevent habituation.
descriptors: They don't, because there are none.
They're repetitive and predictable, so results aren't valid.
Starting to vary in format and complexity, informing priorities.
Timing, context, and complexity varied deliberately to prevent habituation.
Selection and difficulty adapt to data, with overconfidence monitored.

questions.text: How is security content presented to employees?
questions.guidance: Measures narrative relatability versus abstract policy.
descriptors: As rules and checklists, with no narrative.
With generic scenario examples people don't see themselves in.
With some storytelling, like video vignettes of real situations.
Around peer-like characters in realistic dilemmas, as a core principle.
With character and scenario realism tested against outcomes and refined.

questions.text: What role does emotion play in your content design?
questions.guidance: Measures emotional resonance as a deliberate learning mechanism.
descriptors: None; there's no emotional context to aid recall.
It's not treated as a design objective.
Used to boost engagement, but not consistently across formats.
Treated as a functional part of behaviour change across formats.
Tested against retention and behaviour data and tuned to audiences.

questions.text: Our training mostly asks employees to:
questions.guidance: Measures purposeful interactivity versus passive consumption.
descriptors: Read, watch, or click through passively.
Answer quizzes that track completion, not skill.
Work through branching scenarios and make decisions with feedback.
Engage with formats matched deliberately to objective and audience.
Use interactions that adapt to their engagement and skill in real time.

questions.text: How do you keep interactive training from wearing people out?
questions.guidance: Measures whether interactive elements are used in the right amount, so they help people learn rather than tiring them or making them tune out.
descriptors: We don't. There's either too little to hold attention or so much that people switch off.
We add interactive parts because we feel we should, without thinking about whether it's too much.
We pick formats on purpose, but we don't really check whether people find it a slog.
We watch for signs people are getting tired or tuning out, just as closely as we watch engagement.
The amount of interaction adjusts to each person, easing off when someone starts to disengage.

questions.text: How much does your content touch employees' personal and home security?
questions.guidance: Measures personal relevance beyond organisational risk.
descriptors: Not at all; it's framed purely around organisational risk.
Personal examples appear by chance, not by design.
Personal scenarios like safe banking are deliberately included.
Personal and family safety are used as a deliberate engagement strategy.
Personalised to life contexts, with gains in engagement and transfer.

questions.text: How do you use personal relevance to draw employees in?
questions.guidance: Measures sequencing of personal relevance and reciprocity.
descriptors: We don't; there's no sense it drives engagement.
Framing stays corporate; personal relevance isn't an entry point.
Recognised as a driver, but not used systematically up front.
We open with personal safety before moving to workplace behaviours.
Personal entry points are refined and shown to improve transfer to work.

questions.text: How tailored is training to different employee groups?
questions.guidance: Measures role differentiation of scenarios.
descriptors: Not at all; everyone gets the same module.
Uniform and generic to satisfy audits.
Broad tiers get content matched to their risk profile.
Content and depth matched to each group's threats, decisions, and access.
Continuously refined with threat intel and behaviour data as roles change.

questions.text: How do high-value targets (executives, finance, admins) get trained?
questions.guidance: Measures focused attention on elevated-risk roles.
descriptors: The same as everyone else; no special attention.
Acknowledged in policy, but not in training.
They get scenarios reflecting their specific threats.
They get meaningfully differentiated, focused training.
They show measurably better outcomes, with content adapting to their risk.

questions.text: How is your audience divided for training and comms?
questions.guidance: Measures breadth of segmentation dimensions.
descriptors: It isn't; everyone gets identical content.
Only by basic hierarchy and system access.
By role tiers, with functional risk starting to be identified.
Across role, functional risk, culture, environment, and knowledge.
Dynamically, with profiles updating as roles change and gaps appear.

questions.text: How different is what each segment actually receives?
questions.guidance: Measures differentiated delivery per segment.
descriptors: Not different; content and style are uniform.
Barely, and driven by access rather than need.
Differentiated by tier, but not yet by culture or demographics.
Meaningfully differentiated in content and style, high-risk groups identified.
Content, style, and channel adapt per segment with measurable differences.

questions.text: Which best describes how senior leaders talk about cybersecurity?
questions.guidance: Measures whether leaders signal that security matters as a business priority.
descriptors: They rarely mention it, so people assume it doesn't matter.
They mention it only when audits or compliance require.
They name security as a formal priority and explain why it matters.
They speak about it regularly and personally as a shared value.
They frame it as a strategic enabler in a shared language of risk, backed by data.

questions.text: Beyond talking, what do your leaders actually do about security?
questions.guidance: Measures whether leaders model secure behaviour and participate themselves.
descriptors: Nothing visible; they don't follow security practices themselves.
They approve initiatives but don't take part or model the behaviour.
They support initiatives and model expected behaviour when prompted.
They consistently model secure behaviour and join awareness activities.
They treat their own secure behaviour as non-negotiable and actively sponsor it.

questions.text: Which best describes the budget behind your awareness program?
questions.guidance: Measures the financial dimension of resourcing.
descriptors: No budget: we only spend reactively after an incident.
Bare minimum: just enough to satisfy audits.
Dedicated budget: funds role-based training and targeted campaigns.
Sustained investment: funds continuous, behaviour-focused initiatives.
Optimised: we base the budget on the risks it reduces and the value it returns, and we adjust it as results come in.

questions.text: Who runs your security awareness program?
questions.guidance: Measures the personnel dimension of resourcing.
descriptors: No one; activities happen ad hoc with no owner.
Someone does it on the side, as an extra duty with no real time or skill.
Named, skilled staff hold clear program-management responsibility.
Dedicated staff plus a wider champion network deliver across functions.
We focus our skilled people where the risk is greatest, and use automation to extend their reach across the organisation.

questions.text: How much work time do employees actually get for security activities?
questions.guidance: Measures the time dimension of resourcing.
descriptors: None; training loses out to daily work every time.
Just enough, grudgingly, for mandatory training.
We formally allocate work time for required training and activities.
Protected time is built into workflows for training and champion work.
Security time is woven into work through low-friction, just-in-time moments.

questions.text: How actively does senior leadership stand behind the organisation's security policy?
questions.guidance: Measures whether leaders genuinely own and back the security policy, giving it real authority and accountability, rather than rubber-stamping it as a technical document.
descriptors: There's no real security policy, or it's left entirely to the technical team.
Leaders sign it off to meet requirements, but do nothing more with it.
Leaders treat it as important, put someone in charge of it, and give it real authority.
Leaders visibly stand behind it and hold people accountable for following it.
Following the policy is a shared responsibility across leadership, security, and the wider business.

questions.text: How connected is your security policy to the way the business actually runs?
questions.guidance: Measures whether the security policy is woven into business goals and everyday decisions, rather than sitting on its own as a compliance document.
descriptors: There isn't one, or it has nothing to do with what we actually do.
It exists for compliance, separate from real business decisions.
It lines up with our goals and is explained in terms of managing risk.
It's turned into practical expectations for each role and built into how projects run.
It runs through the whole employee journey and our decision-making, and we keep refining it.

questions.text: Who is responsible for information security in your organisation?
questions.guidance: Measures whether responsibility is distributed across the organisation, not just IT.
descriptors: It's unclear, and defaults to IT by assumption.
People follow rules to comply, but ownership is minimal.
Responsibilities are clearly defined and understood across roles.
Responsibility is genuinely shared, with peers reinforcing good practice.
Ownership is distributed and embedded through HRM and performance alignment.

questions.text: When an employee spots a possible security risk, what usually happens?
questions.guidance: Measures active employee participation in identifying and responding to risk.
descriptors: Nothing; risks surface only after an incident, often met with blame.
They act only if required to.
They know how to report it, though participation is task-focused.
They report it and actively support secure behaviour around them.
They act as a proactive sensor network, consistently flagging and helping respond.

questions.text: Asked how they'd feel reporting their own security mistake, most employees would say:
questions.guidance: Measures the felt safety to disclose mistakes without fear of blame.
descriptors: “I'd hide it; owning up gets you blamed.”
“I'd report only if I had to; the risk of sanctions isn't worth it.”
“I know I'm supposed to report it, though it depends on my manager.”
“I'd report it; we're encouraged to and it's treated as normal.”
“I'd report it without a second thought; it's safe and expected here.”

questions.text: When a mistake is reported, how does your organisation respond?
questions.guidance: Measures whether reports drive learning rather than blame.
descriptors: It's treated as an individual failure to be punished.
It's logged for compliance but rarely used to improve anything.
It informs basic improvements and awareness.
It's consistently treated as a learning opportunity that strengthens security.
It feeds rapid feedback loops that measurably improve resilience.

questions.text: Among colleagues, how is secure behaviour treated socially?
questions.guidance: Measures peer-level social influence on secure behaviour.
descriptors: It's not; risky behaviour goes unchallenged.
Any pressure comes from fear of audits, not shared norms.
It's increasingly seen as a workplace standard, with informal sharing.
Peers encourage each other and challenge unsafe practices.
It's a self-reinforcing default; lapses draw immediate peer feedback.

questions.text: Within teams, how do managers handle secure behaviour?
questions.guidance: Measures managerial social influence on secure behaviour.
descriptors: They neither model nor expect it; it's IT's concern.
They enforce rules only to avoid consequences.
They communicate security expectations as part of professional conduct.
They consistently model it and reinforce it as a team standard.
Their modelling is continuous and visible, anchoring it as the norm.

questions.text: For a new joiner, security shows up:
questions.guidance: Measures whether security enters the lifecycle at recruitment and onboarding.
descriptors: Not at all; they get no security grounding.
As a generic, one-off mandatory onboarding module.
As formal onboarding expectations linked to policy.
As onboarding tailored to their role and risk from day one.
From recruitment onward, feeding continuous, risk-aware support.

questions.text: How does security feature in job descriptions and role expectations?
questions.guidance: Measures whether security is built into how roles are defined.
descriptors: It doesn't appear in them at all.
It's referenced generically to meet policy, not built into expectations.
Responsibilities are formally written into job descriptions.
Expectations include security objectives tailored by function and risk.
Expectations adapt continuously to role, access, and risk changes.

questions.text: Across performance reviews, role moves, and exits, how is security handled?
questions.guidance: Measures whether security is sustained through performance and offboarding.
descriptors: It never features; offboarding ignores it.
Any link to performance is nominal and compliance-driven.
It appears in performance talks, with basic steps at transitions.
It's built into appraisals and incentives, with secure transitions and offboarding.
Accountability evolves with the role in real time, including disciplined offboarding.

questions.text: How clearly do employees know the security responsibilities specific to their role?
questions.guidance: Measures clarity of role-tailored security responsibilities.
descriptors: They don't; it all defaults to IT.
They get one-size-fits-all policies not tailored to their role.
Responsibilities are in job descriptions, with extra guidance for higher-risk roles.
Role-specific responsibilities are clear and reinforced by champions.
They adapt continuously to role, access, and emerging risk.

questions.text: Asked who's accountable for the security risks in their daily tasks, most employees would say:
questions.guidance: Measures felt accountability for role-specific risks.
descriptors: “Not me; that's IT's job.”
“Me, but only as far as the rules require.”
“Me, for the sensitive data and systems I handle.”
“Me; it's part of doing my job well.”
“Me, and what I own shifts as my role and access change.”

questions.text: How well do your awareness materials fit employees' actual roles and work?
questions.guidance: Measures relevance of materials to user roles and contexts.
descriptors: Poorly; they're generic and jargon-heavy.
Off-the-shelf content with little relevance to real work.
Basic segmentation and relatable examples improve relevance.
Content is closely matched to roles, contexts, and real decisions.
Content is dynamically personalised to behaviour, role, and risk.

questions.text: How easy are your awareness materials to absorb and act on?
questions.guidance: Measures usability and cognitive-load reduction.
descriptors: Hard; dense one-off sessions that overwhelm.
Built for audit, not usability; load isn't considered.
Clearer, scenario-based formats ease comprehension.
Bite-sized, accessible formats cut load and aid retention.
Intentionally simplified, surfacing the right support at the right moment.

questions.text: Where is your organisation on accessibility for different abilities and assistive tech?
questions.guidance: Measures accessibility for ability and assistive-technology needs.
descriptors: Not considered; some users are effectively excluded.
Minimal alt text or captions, only to meet legal requirements.
Recognised standards (e.g., WCAG) and multiple formats adopted.
Accessibility is embedded in design, tested across user groups.
A core design requirement, with adaptive delivery and accessibility metrics.

questions.text: How well do materials work across cultural, language, and literacy differences?
questions.guidance: Measures inclusivity across cultural, linguistic, and literacy needs.
descriptors: Not at all; one dense format serves everyone.
Inclusivity isn't part of design; content stays standardised.
Language is simplified, with foundational multilingual and multicultural support.
Content reflects diverse representation and is tested across groups.
Inclusive design is continuous and data-informed, with demographic-sensitive metrics.

questions.text: How do you find out how employees really behave day to day?
questions.guidance: Measures discreet observation of real everyday behaviour.
descriptors: We don't; behaviour is unknown until an incident.
Occasional announced audit checks, not real everyday behaviour.
Periodic unannounced walkabouts, documented to find gaps.
Discreet, regular observations generating comparable data over time.
Method and frequency varied deliberately to stay valid and manage culture.

questions.text: What do you do with what you learn from watching how people behave?
questions.guidance: Measures whether observation feeds benchmarks and improvement.
descriptors: Nothing usable comes of it.
It's recorded for compliance, not used to improve.
It's documented and used to spot gaps, but not benchmarked.
It's turned into benchmarks and triangulated with other data.
Benchmarks drive program change, with demonstrable improvement over time.

questions.text: How do you track what your fake phishing tests tell you?
questions.guidance: Measures simulations as a measurement tool tracking click and reporting rates.
descriptors: We don't run them, so vulnerability is unknown, with no baseline.
We run the odd one for click-rate stats, no baseline or reporting rate.
Regular runs tracked against a baseline; reporting rate is creeping in.
Click and reporting rates tracked across cycles as paired indicators.
Results drive adaptation in real time, with difficulty tuned to performance.

questions.text: When someone fails a phishing simulation, what do they get?
questions.guidance: Measures simulations as a corrective learning mechanism.
descriptors: Nothing; there's no intervention at the moment of failure.
Little or nothing; a failure just becomes a statistic.
Corrective training triggered right at the point of the click.
Consistent, meaningful feedback that turns failure into learning.
Adaptive correction tied to performance and the wider program.

questions.text: How varied and realistic are your phishing simulations?
questions.guidance: Measures design validity: realism, variation, and trust.
descriptors: There's no real design to assess.
Generic, repeated templates that are predictable and low-validity.
Templates starting to vary in format and complexity.
Timing, template, and complexity varied to stay realistic.
Design adapts to new phishing trends while protecting employee trust.

questions.text: What do you use to measure knowledge, attitude, and behaviour?
questions.guidance: Measures instrument rigour versus generic feedback forms.
descriptors: Nothing structured.
Basic feedback forms or quizzes, not validated or comparable.
Structured pre/post questionnaires adapted from published sources.
We use a recognised, tested questionnaire consistently across the workforce.
We tailor the questionnaire to our context, repeat it over time, and check it stays accurate.

questions.text: How do you handle the gap between what people say and what they do?
questions.guidance: Measures triangulation of self-report with behavioural data.
descriptors: We don't; there's nothing to triangulate.
We take self-report at face value.
We track results over time but don't yet cross-check with behaviour.
We acknowledge bias and triangulate with simulation and observation data.
Questionnaire scores are shown to track with real behaviour.

questions.text: If reported incidents suddenly rose after a campaign, how would you read it?
questions.guidance: Measures correct interpretation of the reporting paradox.
descriptors: We wouldn't notice; incidents aren't linked to the program.
As a failure, and we don't separate breaches from reports.
Often correctly, as growing vigilance, and we're starting to separate the two.
As vigilance, tracking confirmed breaches and reports separately in context.
As a leading indicator of culture, with breaches read against the threat landscape.

questions.text: How do you use incident data to judge the program?
questions.guidance: Measures triangulation of incident data rather than standalone use.
descriptors: We don't connect it to behaviour or training.
Counts stand alone, with no link to program activity.
Tracked, but not yet combined with behavioural or attitudinal data.
One input among several, avoiding causal claims from counts alone.
Tied to specific interventions over time in an evidence-based account.

questions.text: How do you know whether behaviour has actually changed?
questions.guidance: Measures direct behavioural measurement versus proxies.
descriptors: We don't; success is assumed or undefined.
By completion, attendance, and quiz scores.
By at least one direct indicator, often click rate, alongside knowledge.
From click rates, reporting rates, and audit checks tracked over repeated rounds.
By simulation, audit, and observation data combined into a live dashboard.

questions.text: How complete is your view of behaviour, and what frames it?
questions.guidance: Measures breadth of behaviours and grounding in a framework.
descriptors: No framework, and no sense of the knowing-doing gap.
Just presence and short-term recall.
We're starting to track hidden workarounds and rule-breaking.
Built around a recognised model that looks at people's ability, opportunity, and motivation to change.
We tackle hidden workarounds and can show lasting change across different groups.

questions.text: When do you measure whether training knowledge stuck?
questions.guidance: Measures longitudinal retention versus immediate recall.
descriptors: Never; there's no measurement at all.
Only right after training, treated as proof of learning.
At intervals after training, aggregated via LMS data.
Continuously between training rounds, as a built-in part of the program.
Continuously across the workforce, so we catch fading knowledge before it becomes a risk.

questions.text: Once you know how well knowledge sticks, what do you do with that?
questions.guidance: Measures whether retention data is paired with behaviour and acted on.
descriptors: Nothing; there's no data to act on.
Record it for completion, disconnected from behaviour.
Use trends to inform reinforcement frequency and format.
Let it shape format choices and triangulate with behaviour.
Analyse it with simulation and incident data to predict and pre-empt risk.
